title: The Bank Run
subtitle: Maturity mismatch & the speed of fear

hook.metaphor: A bank takes your deposit (which you can withdraw any time) and lends it out for years (a 30-year mortgage). That's its whole business model — and its fatal flaw. If everyone asks for their money back at once, the cash simply isn't there: it's locked in long-term loans. The bank isn't a vault full of your cash; it's a clever, fragile timing trick. A bank run is that trick failing.
hook.scenario: A perfectly solvent bank's customers hear a rumour it's in trouble. Frightened, they all rush to withdraw at once. The bank can't return everyone's money simultaneously — because it's lent out — so it collapses, *making the rumour come true*. How did a healthy bank die of a rumour?

learn: The engine of bank fragility is **maturity mismatch** (or 'maturity transformation'): banks fund *long-term* assets (loans, mortgages) with *short-term* liabilities (deposits withdrawable on demand). This is genuinely useful — it's how savings become mortgages — but it means a bank can *never* repay all depositors at once.

Normally that's fine, because only a few withdraw at any time. A **bank run** happens when **confidence breaks** and everyone demands their cash together. The bank, unable to instantly recall its long-term loans, runs out of cash and fails — even if it was perfectly solvent.

The cruellest twist is the **self-fulfilling prophecy**: the *fear* of failure causes the failure. A solvent bank dies because people *believed* it might. This makes liquidity uniquely psychological — confidence isn't a nice-to-have, it's the foundation the whole thing stands on.

Modern runs are **faster than ever**. Social media spreads panic in minutes and mobile banking lets billions be withdrawn in hours — as the 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank showed: a digital run that emptied the bank at a speed no traditional run ever matched.

Two big defences exist: **deposit insurance** (governments guaranteeing deposits up to a limit — like Australia's Financial Claims Scheme — so people *don't* panic), and the **central bank as lender of last resort**, which can lend emergency cash to a solvent-but-illiquid bank to break the panic. Both exist to protect *confidence*, because confidence is the real asset.
bridge: So at work, you can understand why liquidity is treated almost as sacred in finance: the bank business model is built on a timing trick that *depends on confidence*, and confidence can evaporate in hours — faster now than ever. It's why regulators obsess over liquidity buffers and deposit insurance: they're really defending the one thing that can't be allowed to break — trust.

interactions.title: Death by rumour
interactions.prompt: A solvent bank collapsed because customers feared it would, and all withdrew at once. How is that possible?

options.label: It must have secretly been insolvent all along.
options.feedback: Not necessarily — a perfectly solvent bank can fail a run. Its money is lent out long-term; it simply can't return all deposits at once. The fear, not insolvency, was fatal.

options.label: Maturity mismatch + lost confidence — the fear became self-fulfilling.
options.feedback: Exactly. Banks fund long-term loans with on-demand deposits, so a simultaneous withdrawal breaks them regardless of solvency. The rumour caused the very collapse it predicted.

options.label: It was a cyber attack.
options.feedback: No systems were hacked. This is a classic liquidity/confidence failure driven by maturity mismatch, not a cyber event.

interactions.title: Bank-run vocabulary
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Funding long-term assets with short-term, on-demand money
Everyone demanding their deposits at once
Fear of failure causing the failure
A guarantee on deposits so people don't panic
A central bank lending emergency cash to break a panic

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A bank runs on a timing trick — long-term loans funded by on-demand deposits — so if confidence breaks and everyone withdraws at once, even a solvent bank collapses, and modern digital runs make that happen in hours.”

options: Funding long-term assets (loans) with short-term, on-demand deposits
Loans and deposits have the same term
checkpoint.explain: It's the core of banking — and the reason a bank can never repay all depositors simultaneously.

checkpoint.q: A bank run can destroy a bank that is…
options: Even a perfectly solvent one, because confidence and timing fail
checkpoint.explain: The fear becomes self-fulfilling: a solvent bank can't return all deposits at once and collapses.

checkpoint.q: Deposit insurance and a lender of last resort both exist mainly to…
options: Protect confidence so panics don't start or spread
checkpoint.explain: Both defend trust — the real foundation liquidity depends on.
